title: Share SSH agent with Podman container on macOS
body:
### Feature request description

Since the move to virtiofs, you can no longer share the SSH agent from the macOS host into a Podman container.  We need a way to share the SSH agent, particularly so that we can handle hardware SSH keys (e.g. YubiKey), where the key isn't available as a file to mount into the container.

### Suggest potential solution

Allow SSH agent (socket) to be shared into a Podman container on macOS.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

Alternative for the YubiKey case could be to expose the USB device into the Podman Machine VM, but that isn't possible with Podman and applehv.

### Additional context

Briefly discussed in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23245

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Another issue, where this was discussed: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14074

While I would like to see a general podman solution as well, here a quick and dirty workaround by remote forwarding the ssh-agent socket via openssh from the podman machine to the local socket:


```
#!/opt/homebrew/bin/bash
MACHINE=testvm
### path to ssh-agent socket within the machine
SASOCK=/tmp/ssh-agent.sock
### get ssh connection string for machine in form: 
### -i <id> ssh://<user>@<host>:<port>
CON=$(podman system connection list \
    | grep "^${MACHINE}[[:space:]]" \
    | awk '{print "-i "$3" "$2}' \
    | sed 's/\/run.*//')

### remove any stale socket
ssh $CON rm -f ${SASOCK}

### forward socket from within machine
### to local ssh-agent socket (macos or brew)
ssh $CON -R ${SASOCK}:${SSH_AUTH_SOCK} -N
```
While the socket forwarding is running:

```
#!/opt/homebrew/bin/bash
### path to ssh-agent socket within the machine
SASOCK=/tmp/ssh-agent.sock
podman run \
    --replace \
    --name test \
    --rm \
    --privileged \
    -v ${SASOCK}:${SASOCK}:z \
    -e SSH_AUTH_SOCK=${SASOCK} \
    -ti \
    docker.io/library/alpine \
   /bin/sh -c 'apk add openssh-client ; ssh-add -l'
```

However, could someone enlighten me, why this container needs to be privileged for accessing the socket? Can it be avoided?

FYI the first script can be a bit simpler by just doing
```
podman machine ssh -- -R /tmp/ssh-agent.sock:"$SSH_AUTH_SOCK" -N
```
not sure why `--priviliged` is needed but `--security-opt label=disable` also works:
```
podman run --rm -it \
    -v /tmp/ssh-agent.sock:/ssh-agent \
    -e SSH_AUTH_SOCK=/ssh-agent \
    --security-opt label=disable \
    docker.io/library/alpine \
    sh -c 'apk add openssh-client && ssh-add -l'
```
Any activity on this? This is a great feature on the docker side of things, and is a massive help for testing remote build setups (that require SSH).
